title: Fairblock
description: Fairblock is a decentralized cryptographic computer with its own execution layer for confidential computing. Confidential apps (cApps) run on Fairblock's native chain, FairyRing, and are accessible from major ecosystems without bridging funds or switching wallets. This evaluation assesses the network, in combination with Stabletrust, Fairblock's confidential stablecoin-transfer application built on FairyRing, which keeps balances and amounts encrypted while leaving addresses transparent.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Account holdings and transfer amounts are encrypted end-to-end. On FairyRing a CosmWasm contract credits the user with an encrypted balance for a deposit, recording only ciphertext on-chain with no plaintext balance visible. Transfers update the encrypted balances of sender and receiver through homomorphic addition and subtraction, never exposing the underlying token amounts. Addresses stay transparent, so confidentiality holds over values while anonymity does not.

citations.cited_text: Only encrypted values are recorded on FairyRing. No plaintext balances are visible onchain.
FairyRing updates the encrypted balances of both sender and the receiver using homomorphic addition/subtraction, without revealing the underlying token amounts.
Amounts and balances are encrypted while addresses stay transparent.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Sender and receiver addresses stay visible on-chain in the confidential-transfer design. Encryption covers amounts and balances by default while addresses are deliberately kept transparent, so observers can still see who transacted with whom. Address-privacy modes exist only as opt-in KYB/KYC-gated environments rather than the default Stabletrust flow.

citations.cited_text:
Compliance and Regulatory Alignment ​ 

Confidential amounts, transparent addresses 

Fairblock encrypts amounts and balances by default, but keeps sender and receiver addresses transparent.
Regulators, auditors, and banks can still see who interacted with who.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Asset identity is exposed at the protocol boundary. FairyRing's confidential ledger keeps encrypted balances per account and per asset, so the asset type is a separate identifiable dimension rather than a hidden attribute. Each integrated EVM chain deploys a minimal escrow contract that locks the underlying token, so the asset moving through a deposit or withdrawal is not itself hidden. Only amounts and balances are encrypted, never the asset identifier.

citations.cited_text: maintain a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset.

System Overview ​ 

Each integrated EVM chain deploys a minimal Solidity escrow contract that locks underlying tokens.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: The entry step is plainly observable on-chain. A first-time user connects a wallet to create a confidential account, then makes an explicit deposit into the escrow to fund it, and that deposit is a distinct protocol interaction rather than an ordinary transfer. Amounts and balances are encrypted while addresses stay transparent, so each confidential interaction remains distinguishable from a plain public stablecoin transfer at both the deposit and transfer-contract level.

citations.cited_text: First-time users connect a wallet (creating a confidential account) and make an initial deposit to fund it.
Amounts and balances are encrypted while addresses stay transparent.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Confidential transfers settle on FairyRing itself, a sovereign Cosmos chain running CometBFT consensus, so a transfer is final on block commit under that chain's own deterministic finality rather than any EVM host's. The EVM host chains only bound the separate deposit and withdrawal legs, which cross over via IBC light clients that verify headers and proofs on incoming packets. No protocol-specific challenge window is added.
properties.needsResearchReview: FairyRing block time not documented in sources — finality in seconds unconfirmed

citations.cited_text: maintain a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset.
Each chain maintains a light client of the other, allowing it to verify headers and proofs accompanying incoming packets.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Beyond the wallet itself the user stores no new secret. The confidential account's homomorphic-encryption keypair is derived deterministically from a wallet signature, never persisted, and re-derived from that signature each session, so it is not an independently held key. First-time users connect a wallet to create the account and fund it with an initial deposit, after which sending and receiving need no further setup.

citations.cited_text: First-time users connect a wallet (creating a confidential account) and make an initial deposit to fund it.
it prompts a wallet signature, derives the HE keypair from that signature, and registers the public key onchain if it does not yet exist.

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Depositing is an ordinary EVM transaction with no protocol-imposed waiting period beyond block inclusion and IBC relay latency. The user submits a standard transaction to the escrow contract sending the tokens, the escrow locks them and builds an IBC packet, and a CosmWasm contract on FairyRing verifies it and credits an encrypted balance. No queue, challenge window, or time-lock gates entry.

citations.cited_text: To enter the confidential system, a user submits a standard EVM transaction to the escrow contract, sending the desired amount of tokens.
A CosmWasm contract receives the packet, verifies it, and credits the user with an encrypted balance representing the deposited amount.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: Withdrawal completes within a single round-trip, with no protocol-imposed waiting period. The user submits a transaction to the escrow contract with the withdrawal amount and a ZK proof of sufficient confidential balance, a relayer forwards it to FairyRing where a CosmWasm contract verifies the proof and updates the encrypted balance, then a response packet returns and the escrow releases the locked tokens. No challenge window, exit queue, or time-lock gates redemption.

citations.cited_text: they can submit a transaction to the escrow contract with the withdrawal amount and a ZK proof showing sufficient confidential balance.
A relayer forwards the packet to FairyRing, where the CosmWasm contract verifies the proof and updates the encrypted balance accordingly.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The initial release runs as a Proof of Authority chain, so a permissioned validator set produces blocks and can withhold inclusion of valid transactions. Decryption and execution also depend on validator participation, since at least 2/3 + 1 of validators must submit keyshares to form the aggregated keyshare, letting the authority set block execution by refusing to contribute shares. Simple slashing mechanisms for misbehaviour exist.

citations.cited_text: The initial release will be a Proof of Authority (PoA) chain similar to the approach used by Noble
Currently, at least 2/3 + 1 of the validators have to submit keyshares to create the aggregated keyshare.
There are some simple slashing conditions implemented for now (such as submitting incorrect keyshares, or skipping blocks), but this may change.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: No external network with added crypto-economic assumptions. The only cross-chain link, used for deposits and withdrawals, is IBC, which is trust-minimised and permissionless: light clients verify incoming packets, relayers cannot forge them, and security rests on the connected chains' own consensus rather than any custodian. The protocol adds no trusted relayer set, oracle, or external validator network beyond the public chains a user already transacts on.

citations.cited_text: Currently, at least 2/3 + 1 of the validators have to submit keyshares to create the aggregated keyshare.
The initial release will be a Proof of Authority (PoA) chain similar to the approach used by Noble
Relayers - The communication method that listens and coordinates between the FairyRing and destination chains for transaction decryption and execution. An example of this includes FairyPort and IBC.
Off-chain relayers merely move packets between chains; they do not participate in validation and cannot forge valid headers, tamper with proofs, or undetectably alter packet contents.
the security of cross-chain communication is ultimately anchored in the consensus of the connected chains rather than in any external custodian or relayer.
IBC is secure and permissionless.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Redeeming a shielded balance requires a transaction to the escrow contract carrying a ZK proof of sufficient balance, wrapped into an IBC packet and forwarded to FairyRing before any tokens unlock. FairyRing's CosmWasm contract verifies the proof and returns a response packet, after which the escrow releases the locked tokens. Exit depends on the PoA validator set producing that response and on relayers transporting it, with no path relying solely on the host chain's consensus.

citations.cited_text: they can submit a transaction to the escrow contract with the withdrawal amount and a ZK proof showing sufficient confidential balance.
A relayer forwards the packet to FairyRing, where the CosmWasm contract verifies the proof and updates the encrypted balance accordingly.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: FairyRing is a Cosmos chain, so protocol changes to its modules ship through coordinated chain upgrades rather than a proxy admin, with the Proof of Authority validator set coordinating any binary upgrade. The host-chain escrow contracts on partner EVM chains follow those chains' own upgrade mechanics, but the confidential computation lives natively on FairyRing and changes by fork.
properties.needsResearchReview: host-chain escrow contract admin slot not classified on-chain — EOA vs multisig upgrade authority unverified

citations.cited_text: The FairyRing network is a Cosmos chain itself.
The initial release will be a Proof of Authority (PoA) chain similar to the approach used by Noble

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Proof generation runs on the user's device, with ZK proofs light enough to execute inside the normal payment flow rather than taking minutes on a phone or needing heavyweight off-chain computation. The design avoids outsourced ZK provers and centralized off-chain coprocessors, keeping witness generation local to the sender instead of a hosted service.

citations.cited_text: ZK proofs are light enough to run in normal payment flow.
No black-box trust : Users are not exposed to unnecessary security risks from single hardware setups (e.g. single-TEE), outsourced ZK provers, or centralized offchain coprocessors.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Balances are encrypted on FairyRing as ciphertext. Each user's homomorphic-encryption key is derived from their wallet signature and never leaves the browser, so no operator reads balances by default and there is no persistent audit key. But FairyRing's threshold identity-based encryption lets the validator set produce scoped keys that reveal specific accounts or transactions under defined conditions for compliance, audit, or investigation.

citations.cited_text: Only encrypted values are recorded on FairyRing. No plaintext balances are visible onchain.
Users decrypt their own balances locally with their own keys, while only encrypted amounts are stored onchain.
When required for compliance, audits, or investigations, authorized parties can be granted access to scoped decryption keys.
This is enabled by FairyRing’s threshold identity-based encryption (IBE), which allows specific accounts or transaction subsets to be revealed under well-defined conditions

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: FairyRing's public testnet has been live since 7 March 2024 and has iterated to fairyring-testnet-3, but no permissionless mainnet exists yet. The initial production release is described as a forthcoming Proof of Authority chain. The Stabletrust confidential-transfer interface targets EVM chains plus FairyRing, with Solana and Stellar listed as upcoming, placing the product ahead of a proven mainnet deployment.

citations.cited_text: The initial release will be a Proof of Authority (PoA) chain similar to the approach used by Noble
Chains: Major EVM chains and payment networks (Ethereum, Arbitrum, Base, Tempo, Arc, Noble AppLayer, Stable), FairyRing, and soon: Solana, Stellar, and others.
Fairblock Public Testnet is Now Live

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: The cryptographic stack rests on classical hardness assumptions. Confidential transfers use additive homomorphic computation and fast zero-knowledge proof verification, and selective disclosure rests on threshold identity-based encryption, a pairing-based construction. None of these named primitives are lattice or hash-based post-quantum schemes, so a Shor-capable adversary could break both ciphertext confidentiality and proof soundness.

citations.cited_text: Instead, confidential transfers use lightweight additive homomorphic calculations and fast zero-knowledge (ZK)-proof verification, executed within the protocol flow.
This is enabled by FairyRing’s threshold identity-based encryption (IBE), which allows specific accounts or transaction subsets to be revealed under well-defined conditions

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Enforcement sits at the chain layer through FairyRing's threshold identity-based encryption. The validator committee can produce scoped decryption keys that reveal specific accounts or transactions under defined conditions for compliance, audit, or investigation — a capability built into the network rather than an individual app or asset. External analytics screening is referenced separately for flagging risky addresses but is not itself a transfer gate.

citations.cited_text: When required for compliance, audits, or investigations, authorized parties can be granted access to scoped decryption keys.
Fairblock uses identity-based encryption and multiparty computation (IBE-MPC) to enable per-transaction disclosures.
We work with services like Chainalysis and Range Security to flag and block high-risk or sanctioned addresses.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: The threshold decryption that enables disclosure is performed by FairyRing's Proof of Authority validator committee, a curated set maintained by the core team rather than open governance or an external vendor. Producing a scoped decryption key requires at least 2/3 + 1 of these validators to submit keyshares, so the enforcing entity is the permissioned operator committee.

citations.cited_text: Currently, at least 2/3 + 1 of the validators have to submit keyshares to create the aggregated keyshare.
The initial release will be a Proof of Authority (PoA) chain similar to the approach used by Noble

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Two mechanisms apply. Selective disclosure reveals per-transaction details to auditors, banks, or regulators — sometimes user-initiated, sometimes via the validator committee's threshold decryption under defined conditions — with no single key covering all transactions.

citations.cited_text: Disclose per-transaction details to finance, auditors, banks, or regulators only when needed (no single decrytion key for all transactions)
Fairblock uses identity-based encryption and multiparty computation (IBE-MPC) to enable per-transaction disclosures.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: Disclosure targets the confidential transfer: the threshold decryption reveals specific transactions, and a user can disclose a chosen transaction to an auditor or regulator. Deposits and withdrawals cross the transparent EVM escrow, where addresses are already visible. Address-level AML screening runs through external providers outside this lifecycle.

citations.cited_text: Fairblock uses identity-based encryption and multiparty computation (IBE-MPC) to enable per-transaction disclosures.
We work with services like Chainalysis and Range Security to flag and block high-risk or sanctioned addresses.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users hold the decryption capability for their own balances, with browser-based encryption and decryption performed locally, and can voluntarily disclose a chosen transaction to a tax authority, regulator, or auditor via a scoped view-key. Beyond consent, FairyRing's threshold IBE lets the validator committee grant authorized parties scoped decryption keys under defined conditions for compliance, audit, or investigation.

citations.cited_text: You can disclose a specific transaction – and only that transaction – to a tax authority, regulator, or auditor without revealing your entire financial history.
If access is required, a scoped view-key can be issued for that specific transaction
When required for compliance, audits, or investigations, authorized parties can be granted access to scoped decryption keys.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Viewing permissions are issued per transaction on demand rather than fixed at transfer time. Identity-based encryption and multiparty computation enable per-transaction disclosures, and a scoped view-key can be issued for one specific transaction if access is required. The holder grants access ad hoc per transaction with no single decryption key for all transactions, allowing retroactive scoped disclosure to auditors or regulators without locking viewer identity into the original transfer.

citations.cited_text: Fairblock uses identity-based encryption and multiparty computation (IBE-MPC) to enable per-transaction disclosures.
If access is required, a scoped view-key can be issued for that specific transaction
Disclose per-transaction details to finance, auditors, banks, or regulators only when needed (no single decrytion key for all transactions)

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Transaction correctness on FairyRing is enforced by on-chain zero-knowledge proof verification in CosmWasm contracts: each transfer proves the sender's balance is sufficient, total value is preserved, and no negative balance results. Only valid proofs let FairyRing update encrypted balances, so correctness rests on proof math, not a single TEE or an MPC honest-majority assumption. FairyRing is itself a sovereign Cosmos chain, so ordering and inclusion rely on its own CometBFT consensus.

citations.cited_text: These proofs attest, among other things, that the sender’s balance is sufficient, that the transfer preserves total value, and that no negative balances are created as a result of the operation.
a CosmWasm contract verifies the ZK proofs. If the proofs are valid, FairyRing updates
This design avoids relying on a single trusted execution environment (TEE) or MPC honest-majority assumptions.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The confidential-transfer logic is not openly licensed. FairyRing's core chain is published under Apache-2.0, but the components that implement confidential transfers — the DistributedIBE crypto library and the confidential-transfer CosmWasm contracts — ship without any LICENSE file, leaving them all-rights-reserved by default. The privacy-critical code a user relies on is therefore not open source.

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: FairyRing's CosmWasm contracts keep a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset, updated through ZK proof verification and lightweight homomorphic addition and subtraction so balance changes stay internally consistent without exposing amounts. On-chain state scales with the number of holders and assets — a transfer between existing accounts rewrites their encrypted entries in place rather than appending a new commitment or nullifier per transaction.

citations.cited_text: maintain a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset.
FairyRing updates the encrypted balances of both sender and the receiver using homomorphic addition/subtraction, without revealing the underlying token amounts.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: The confidential ledger stores encrypted balances per account and per asset, with CosmWasm contracts updating entries through ZK proof verification and lightweight homomorphic operations, so a device reads its own account entry rather than trial-decrypting a commitment set. Users decrypt their own balances locally with their own keys against on-chain ciphertext.

citations.cited_text: maintain a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset.
Users decrypt their own balances locally with their own keys, while only encrypted amounts are stored onchain.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: FairyRing keeps a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset in CosmWasm contracts, updated through ZK proof verification and lightweight homomorphic addition and subtraction so balances stay internally consistent without exposing amounts. Transfers update sender and receiver ciphertexts in place rather than spending notes — valid proofs let FairyRing adjust both encrypted balances homomorphically. There is no commitment tree or nullifier set.

citations.cited_text: maintain a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset.
FairyRing updates the encrypted balances of both sender and the receiver using homomorphic addition/subtraction, without revealing the underlying token amounts.

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: Encrypted balances live on FairyRing as a confidential ledger held by CosmWasm contracts, with only ciphertext written on-chain — a deposit credits an encrypted balance and no plaintext is visible. The contracts keep these balances per account and per asset, processed through ZK proof verification and homomorphic operations. The host-chain escrow holds a mirrored view after IBC responses, so the authoritative private data sits in smart-contract storage on both sides.

citations.cited_text: Only encrypted values are recorded on FairyRing. No plaintext balances are visible onchain.
maintain a confidential ledger of encrypted balances per account and per asset.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
value: Composable interface, but requires DeFi protocol changes
properties.notes: The confidential balance's only on-balance action is transfer between confidential accounts — reaching external DeFi means redeeming back to the public EVM chain. FairyRing hosts its own native confidential apps (cApps). Broader composability comes via FairyKit, where an external protocol integrates Fairblock to add confidentiality, so external access requires the protocol to adopt Fairblock's toolkit.

citations.cited_text: receive a confidential balance from FairyRing, perform confidential transactions with that balance, and optionally redeem back to the EVM chain when withdrawing.

Confidential Transactions ​ 

Once the user has an encrypted balance, they can transfer to other confidential accounts while still transacting on the EVM chain.
Natively, developers can build confidential applications (cApps) directly on FairyRing. FairyRing is a self-sufficient chain that can host its own native cApps.
Integration packages that plug into any protocol and application to bring one-click confidentiality to any app or user requiring it.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.value: Full programmability with public and private state
properties.notes: FairyRing is a decentralized cryptographic computer with an execution layer for confidential computing, hosting cApps well beyond transfers. Developers build natively — protected trading with encrypted orders, intents, and bids, plus lending and token launches — and the platform selects a cryptographic scheme per use case (HE, IBE, MPC, lightweight ZK). It runs both public and confidential state, a general programmable confidential computer rather than a fixed payment flow.

citations.cited_text: Fairblock is a dynamic, decentralized cryptographic computer with its own execution layer purpose-built for confidential computing.
Encrypted orders, intents, and bids with conditional decryption enable better price discovery in trading, lending, intents, and token launches
Dynamic confidentiality selects the right cryptographic schemes per use case (HE, IBE, MPC, lightweight ZK, etc.)
